explicit_text: The SNCC house in Albany was where we often gathered, and I remember the food we had there was mostly from battered cans Giles sent over from his grocery. Sherrod would peel off the labels so no one would just eat the lasagna first and leave the string beans. The SNCC folks all remember those bent-up cans of food that kept us going during the Movement in the 1960s. SNCC was central to the civil rights activities in Albany, and we relied on support from people like Giles behind the scenes.
implicit_text: The house where we often gathered in Albany was where we shared meals mostly from battered cans sent over by a local grocer. Everyone involved in the civil rights activities in Albany during the 1960s remembers those bent-up cans of food that kept us going. This group was central to the civil rights efforts in Albany, relying on support from people like that grocer behind the scenes.

explicit_text: Giles was a good old fellow who owned a grocery store and helped the Movement quietly by sending us canned goods with no labels at the SNCC house. When the call came to hire black cashiers, Giles was the first to do so, hiring a fine-looking black woman and having his wife help her with the register. Unlike other grocers like Carl Smith and Earl Bronson, Giles was polite and cared for the welfare folk, selling on credit and supporting us behind the scenes. I knew Giles a long time, borrowed from him, and he was always there for the Movement even if he wasn’t part of the downtown redneck group.
implicit_text: There was a good old fellow who owned a grocery store and helped the Movement quietly by sending us canned goods with no labels at the house where we gathered. Unlike other grocers in town, he was polite and cared for the welfare folk, selling on credit and supporting us behind the scenes. I knew him a long time, borrowed from him, and he was always there for the Movement even if he wasn’t part of the downtown redneck group.

explicit_text: Carl Smith was a grocer in Albany where I worked as a bag boy when I was about fifteen or sixteen in the 1950s. He was a tough cracker, worse than most, and when the Movement boycotted his store for not hiring black help, he fought back hard. Smith was also a juror on the grand jury that acquitted Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson after he tried to kill Charlie Ware, and this led to the Movement being indicted for jury tampering during the boycott. Eventually, the Movement put Carl Smith out of business, but not without a lot of trouble and legal battles.

explicit_text: Earl Bronson owned a grocery store in Albany where I also worked as a bag boy and stock boy after my time at Carl Smith’s. Bronson was one of the grocers who refused to hire black cashiers and was connected to the redneck crowd downtown. I remember working there until the day we jumped in the pool at Tift Park, which ended my little career as a bag boy. Bronson’s grocery was part of the local business scene during the civil rights struggles in Albany.

explicit_text: Sherrod was one of the SNCC folks who helped out by peeling the labels off the battered cans of food Giles sent us at the SNCC house in Albany. That way, no one would just eat the lasagna first and leave the string beans behind. Sherrod’s small but important role helped keep us fed during the Movement’s difficult days in the 1960s. Everyone at SNCC remembers those cans and Sherrod’s part in making sure the food was shared fairly.

explicit_text: Charlie Ware was a small but rugged black man in Baker County who was nearly killed by Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson in 1963 because of a love triangle involving a black woman. The sheriff caught Charlie coming from the woman’s house, handcuffed him, and shot him twice in the head, claiming Charlie tried to choke him. Charlie was rushed to Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital where he barely survived and later sued Baker County. He bought a house in Albany and ran a Shell station on Newton Road, but the sheriff got off free thanks to a jury that included Carl Smith, whose grocery the Movement was boycotting.

He was rushed to the hospital where he barely survived and later sued the county. He bought a house in Albany and ran a gas station on Newton Road, but the sheriff got off free thanks to a jury that included the grocer whose store the Movement was boycotting.

explicit_text: Warren 'Gator' Johnson was the sheriff in Baker County and a real big, tough white man who made life hard for black folks during the 1960s. He was about six-six and 250 pounds, and he made money by giving $35 speeding tickets in Newton, Georgia, even if you were driving exactly the speed limit. He tried to kill Charlie Ware in 1963 over a woman, and though he got off free, the Movement fought back hard. Warren Johnson’s brother Ben was in the army, and Warren even had his brother’s wife living with him, which was part of the complicated family story in those days.

explicit_text: Baker County was where a lot of the tough struggles happened during the civil rights Movement, especially around 1963. It was the place where Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson tried to kill Charlie Ware over a woman, and where the black deputy would inform on the black community. The county had a reputation for harsh policing and racial violence, but it was also where the Movement fought hard for justice. Places like Newton in Baker County were notorious for unfair speeding tickets and racial intimidation.

explicit_text: Albany was the center of much of the civil rights activity I was involved in during the 1960s. It was where the SNCC house was, where we ate those battered canned goods from Giles, and where the Movement boycotted grocers like Carl Smith and Earl Bronson. Albany was also where Charlie Ware bought a house and ran a Shell station after surviving an assassination attempt by Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson. The Albany Movement was a big part of the struggle to end segregation and injustice in Southwest Georgia.

explicit_text: The Albany Movement was the civil rights campaign in Albany, Georgia, during the 1960s that fought segregation and racial injustice. We organized boycotts against grocers like Carl Smith who refused to hire black cashiers and supported the Movement. The Albany Movement also dealt with legal battles, including indictments for jury tampering related to the Charlie Ware case. It was a tough time, but the Movement eventually put some of those businesses out of operation and made progress toward equality.

explicit_text: Camilla was the town where the black funeral home was located that helped save Charlie Ware after he was shot by Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson in 1963. When the ambulance from Camilla arrived to take Charlie’s body, they noticed he was still alive and rushed him to Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital. Camilla played a small but important role in that story, as the place where the black community intervened to save Charlie’s life. It was part of the broader geography of the civil rights struggles in Southwest Georgia.

explicit_text: Phoebe Putney Memorial Hospital in Albany was where Charlie Ware was taken after the black funeral home in Camilla found he was still alive following the shooting by Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson. C.B. met Charlie at the hospital and they operated on him to save his life. The hospital was a critical place in the story of the Movement and the fight against racial violence in the early 1960s. It was where justice and survival intersected for Charlie Ware.

explicit_text: My whole family would go once a month to our church over in Elmerdale, which was a Sanctified church. We were sanctified people, though I wasn’t really part of it myself. To get there, the shortest way was through Newton in Baker County, but we avoided that route because Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson was there and made life hard with his speeding tickets and harassment. The Sanctified church was a part of our community life and faith during those difficult times in the 1960s.

Elmerdale was part of the rural landscape of our lives in Southwest Georgia during the Movement.

explicit_text: Newton was a small town in Baker County where Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson was known for giving unfair $35 speeding tickets to black folks, even if they drove exactly the speed limit. The town had a sign for 15 miles per hour, but Johnson would ticket you for going 14 or 16 miles per hour, making money off poor people. We avoided going through Newton when traveling to Elmerdale for church because of this harassment. Newton was a symbol of the racial oppression and corruption we faced in the 1960s.

We avoided going through this town when traveling to the nearby church because of this harassment. It was a symbol of the racial oppression and corruption we faced in the 1960s.

explicit_text: Ben Johnson was the brother of Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson and served in the United States Army for two years. While Ben was away, Warren 'Gator' Johnson had Ben’s wife living with him and even had a baby by her named 'Screws' Johnson. During the Movement days, Ben became a drunk and would hang out at the Coca-Cola stand in Newton, but if we bought him a pint of wine, he’d share all the gossip and plans of the white folks against the Movement. Ben was an unexpected source of information for us in those tense times.

explicit_text: Back then, when you joined the United States Army, you went for two years straight without coming home for leave. Ben Johnson, brother of Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson, served in the Army for that full term. His absence led to Warren Johnson having Ben’s wife live with him, which was part of the complicated family dynamics in Baker County. The Army service was a significant part of life for young men like Ben during the 1960s.

explicit_text: Charlie Ware ran a Shell station on Newton Road in Albany after surviving the assassination attempt by Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson in 1963. The station was part of his new life after he bought a house in Albany with the settlement from suing Baker County. The Shell station was a symbol of Charlie’s survival and resilience during the civil rights struggles in Southwest Georgia. It was a place where the community could see a black man standing strong despite the violence he faced.

explicit_text: Tift Park in Albany was where I worked as a bag boy and stock boy at Earl Bronson’s grocery until the day we jumped in the pool there, which ended my little career. It was a place where we had some fun amidst the struggles of the Movement in the 1960s. Tift Park was part of the local community life, a spot where young folks like me could escape for a moment from the harsh realities we faced.
